filled.diagnosis_and_prognosis: The claimant has sustained soft tissue injuries to the neck, bilateral shoulders (with the right shoulder being more significantly affected), lower back, and left foot as a result of the index incident. The claimant also reports right-sided sciatica symptoms, which may represent an exacerbation of his pre-existing lumbar disc disease. Radiological investigations including X-rays of the pelvis and left foot, as well as a trauma CT scan, revealed no abnormalities. It is anticipated that with appropriate physiotherapy to the right shoulder, neck, and lower back, the claimant's symptoms should return to a level as close to his pre-accident baseline as possible. However, there may be approximately a ten per cent residual deficit in respect of his ongoing symptoms on a chronic basis.
filled.effect_on_life: The claimant was unable to ride his motorbike for approximately three weeks following the incident, during which time he repaired the vehicle himself. The claimant was also unable to drive during this period. The claimant was required to utilise three days of emergency holiday for recovery purposes, which he ordinarily reserves for genuine emergencies. The claimant did not miss any planned leisure activities or holidays. There was no change to his living arrangements.
filled.effect_on_work: The claimant is employed as a postman, a role which involves significant carrying, lifting, and walking approximately 7.6 miles per day, including tower block deliveries. Following the incident, the claimant attended work the next day (Saturday) on light/amended duties for a total of three days. He was then required to take three days of emergency holiday (Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday) to recover. Thereafter, he worked two further days before a scheduled long weekend (Saturday, Sunday, Monday). Following this, the claimant returned to full unrestricted duties. The claimant was unable to take further time off work as he would not be paid, and he is the sole breadwinner for his household. He persevered with his role despite ongoing symptoms.
examination_findings: Left foot: Full range of dorsiflexion and plantarflexion observed with no pain on movement. The claimant reported intermittent pain across the left ankle on the day of examination, though this was of uncertain aetiology.
Lumbar spine: Forward flexion achieved to just below the knees. Lateral flexion reached just above the knees bilaterally. Rotation of the spine was normal and pain-free.

Right shoulder: Forward flexion limited to approximately 100 degrees. Abduction limited to approximately 100 degrees. External rotation reduced to approximately 70 per cent of normal. Internal rotation limited to T12 level. Pain elicited over the anterior shoulder, coracoid process, and posterior shoulder including the supraspinatus fossa. Clicking noted on certain movements, though this was reported as pre-existing.

Left shoulder: Forward flexion to approximately 180 degrees. Abduction to 180 degrees. External rotation 100 per cent of normal. Internal rotation to T5 level. No pain on examination of the left shoulder.

Cervical spine: Forward flexion elicited pain over the right trapezius. Extension produced slight pain again over the right trapezius. Lateral rotation to the right caused right pectoralis pain. Lateral flexion to the right also caused right pectoralis pain. No pain on direct palpation of the neck. Tenderness noted over the right trapezius and possibly the right periscapular muscles. The left side appeared normal.

Right paraspinal region and right sacroiliac joint: The claimant reported pain in these areas, particularly during work activities. Numbness and tingling reported down the right leg, predominantly in the mornings, consistent with sciatic symptoms.
filled.immediate_medical_attendance: The claimant initially attended Trafford General Hospital on the day of the incident, accompanied by his wife. Due to the incident being classified as major trauma, the claimant was referred to Wythenshawe Hospital, to which he travelled by car. At Wythenshawe, the claimant was triaged and underwent X-rays of both hips and the left foot, which revealed no abnormalities. The claimant was then placed on a trauma/spinal board with foam blocks and head strapping for approximately three and a half hours. A trauma CT scan was also performed, which detected no abnormalities. The claimant was reassured that his injuries were likely musculoskeletal in nature and was advised he would be sore for one to two weeks. He was subsequently discharged.
filled.incident_description: The claimant was riding his motorbike and joining the M60 motorway at junction nine, Trafford Park. Having come off the slip road into the first lane, the claimant attempted to accelerate in order to indicate and move into the middle lane. As he applied power, the rear wheel of the motorbike slipped from underneath him. The claimant and the motorbike spun round and slid backwards into the hard shoulder. The road conditions were damp at the time. Upon subsequent inspection, the claimant observed a significant line of diesel on the road surface, which he believes caused the loss of control. The claimant estimates his speed at the time was approximately 60 miles per hour. The claimant was wearing full safety gear including a helmet at the time of the incident. A member of the public stopped to assist the claimant and helped him pick up the motorbike. The claimant's wife arranged for recovery of the motorbike to the claimant's home address.
filled.living_arrangements: No change to living arrangements as a result of the incident.
filled.medical_history: The claimant has a pre-existing history of bilateral shoulder bursitis and lumbar disc disease affecting the lower back.
physical_symptoms: Left foot pain: Onset on the day of the incident when the motorbike landed on the claimant's left foot. Initially mild to moderate in severity. Resolved to normal within approximately one week. The claimant reported intermittent pain across the left ankle on the day of examination, though this was of uncertain origin.

Lower back pain with right-sided sciatica: Onset on the day of the incident following landing and sliding on his back. Initially moderate in severity. Currently mild and intermittent. The claimant reports pain in the right paraspinal region and right sacroiliac joint area, with numbness and tingling down the right leg predominantly in the mornings, consistent with sciatic symptoms. These symptoms are exacerbated by work activities.
Neck pain: Onset the day after the incident. Initially moderate in severity. Resolved to normal within approximately three weeks.

Bilateral shoulder pain: Onset the day after the incident. Initially moderate in severity. The right shoulder remains symptomatic at a mild level with pain over the anterior shoulder, coracoid process, and supraspinatus fossa, with restricted range of movement. The left shoulder has returned to normal. The claimant has pre-existing bilateral shoulder bursitis, but reports that some of the current right shoulder pains are different from those previously experienced.
filled.pre_existing_conditions: The claimant has a pre-existing history of bilateral shoulder bursitis and lumbar disc disease affecting the lower back.
filled.psychological_symptoms: The claimant does not report any significant psychological symptoms. He denies experiencing flashbacks, nightmares, or clinical depression as a result of the incident. He reports that he has vivid recollection of the incident in slow motion but does not find this distressing. No referral to a psychologist is considered necessary. The claimant has decided not to ride his motorbike on motorways in future, restricting use to scenic routes only.
filled.rehabilitative_treatment: The claimant has not undergone any physiotherapy or attended his general practitioner since the incident. It is recommended that the claimant undergo physiotherapy for his right shoulder, neck, and lower back/sciatica symptoms. With appropriate rehabilitation, it is anticipated that the claimant's symptoms should return to as close to his pre-accident baseline as possible, with an estimated ten per cent residual deficit.
filled.subsequent_medical_attendance: The claimant has not attended his general practitioner or undergone any physiotherapy following discharge from Wythenshawe Hospital. He returned to work the day after the incident.
